The "fixed price" is not always the fixed price. A number of times I've been "just looking" and if you idly express an interest but don't put on the "I want" look then many will drop the price. Don't haggle with them, let them keep offering a lower value until you think it's fair. It's not about getting the cheapest, it's about what you think is a fair price then both parties are happy. If you ever return many have surprisingly long memories and will give you better deals. I've picked up deals not to be missed when I'd intended to look at them properly later on in my journey. It's part of the culture to sit and have a mint tea in the Medinas if offered. They are out to sell you something but it can be worth the money to sit, chat and let the world go by if you are interested in something. They sometimes offer a price and the longer you stay they keep dropping the price until they think they have you on the hook. Use this to your advantage. I find that is particularly the case with those selling fantastic spices. If you drop a hint that you may be idly looking so something else if you pay a fair price they often introduce them to a fellow stallholder and haggle for you. I've had some fantastic deals. Don't commit that you definitely want something, they may take advantage of that. It's all a game of poker and sometimes just saying little gets you a better deal. They will prey on tourists actively looking rather than musing. The price is not what they ask rather than what is it worth to you. That's why you've got to go in knowing how much things should be. If they touch you shake them off and just deliberately walk away. Use your body language that you will not tolerate it.

One tip: don't get absolutely plastered in any of the cities in Morocco. Staggering around will attract the attention of the Service Auxiliares and Police, and you'll be carted off to jail before you can even say "another pint of Casablanca". Believe me, 8 hours in a Moroccan cop shop being interrogated and thrown in a cell with the dregs isn't fun.
If you're a Brit looking for a fun boozy holiday, this ain't the place!

I used debit lots, passport control took less than 30 minutes. Use the bank machines that are yellow, no service charges. If you are a man you can find alcohol everywhere. If you are a woman, go to a Hammam.Bring earplugs if you don't want to wake up to the first call to prayer.

If you're paying by card ALWAYS check the price on the device. They might show you proper price and then change it to higher one. When they try to not show you the price by holding the machine at an angle, they're definitely scamming you.

Local food stalls sell meals for 13 dirham. Just head off the main shopping streets to find cheaper food. Their menus also have fixed pricing list. 1,5 liters water bottle costs 4.5 dirham in Carrefour, but they'll ususlly charge 10 or 20 dirham at restaurants. Scarf prices start at 50 dirham in Essaouira. Same scarf starting price is 150-250 dirham in Marrakech. Just to let you know that no one sells anything unique. There's always someone selling the same thing somewhere. Even handmade Senegalese unique fabrics.

You are way off the mark for the most part. I have been there so many times.
1. International Airports are always busy  and this is no exception.
2. Who the fuck takes drones on holiday. Many people take golf clubs.
3. All cards a re accepted. Capital One/ Santander egs. Amd you speak rubbish. Taxi drivers prefer cash but clubs vars restaurants ALL TAKE CARDS. Yes tips are cash. 20 dirham is £1.60. 50 dirham works out around £4 And so on. And if you are on holday you will not expect to get change from small amounts anyway.
4 Mosques are ok to admire from the outside including The Koutoubia Mosque. Saadian Tombs, Badii Palace, Bahia Palace, and Ben Youssef Madrasa, that are open to the public. But hey who visits mosques in this country - UNLESS YOU ARE MUSLIM. The one in Casablanca is awesome and you can walk through it and straight right on down to the beach at the other end. Didn't tell us that did you?
5 And definitely nothing closes on Fridays. Tourism is central to the economy. Only during RAMADAN mate.  You are speaking through a hole in your A.
France colonised Morocco back in the day and a knowledge of French is advised. But tourism is key to the economy and English is widely recognised some better than others. The English take things for granted when visiting other countries and there is a general acceptance that everyone should be able to speak English. 
6. Water? No matter where you go in the world it is the done thing to drink bottled water anyway. Even here in the UK we tend to drink gallons of bottled water ANYWAY!!!! Tap water is not safe in Morocco mate and I have still to meet someone here who drinks it. Including the locals.
7. Toilet paper I agree with that.
8. Pickpockets are EVERYWHERE in the world. Ever read Charles Dickens??? Morocco is preparing for huge sporting events in the near future and already there is strong evidence of police controlling things and if someone is caught with for example, a knife, expect harsh penalties. King Mohammed has issued instructions especially to safeguard the tourists. There is a lot of poverty in the country by the way. The people are wonderful and friendly and their infectious smile is a blessing. Of course try not go out on your own at night. But that equally applies to anywhere in the world. I live in London's Maida Vale.
9. Yes there are guys who will take advantage but just walk away. Avoid eye contact.
10. Watch yourself when trying to cross the roads. Bikes everywhere. Horse drawn carts barrelling along sometimes behind you so you need to be alert and aware. And do not be surprised to see a family of four on a scooter or a motor bike. I kid you not. Papa with baby in front. ,Mama at the back with another child sandwiched between her and papa. And they do not wear head gear.

I just have a few remarks: 

1. Yes, be careful with photographing. But m it’s similar as in Europe: if you ask kindly the people are mostly uncomplicated. 
They might ask for a tip for being photographed, though 😉

2. Never, I say never! Film military sites, no matter how pittoresque they look. 
You can get into real trouble. Especially at the Borders in Melilla and Ceuta. 

3. About the taxis: the usual price with taxis that have no taximeter (and that’s nearly everyone in Marrakesh, for example) the usual price (again in Marrakesh) for a trip from the station to Jema el Fnaa or from Jema el Fnaa to Gueliz is 35 to 40 dirham. Just that you get an idea. 

4. Yes there are pickpockets, but don’t be afraid. 
Theft or harder crime is a super rare. Simply because of the horrible penalties. 
To give you an idea: a simple theft brings one to jail for 15 years. And: the biggest cities, especially tourist areas, are surveyed by cameras completely. 
If anything should happen, the police is always helpful. 

5. I take one card with me to Morocco only, one that I’m sure it will work there. 
As for the rest of the time I’m sending myself money at Western Union. That’s safe and in my experience super easy. 

And something general, that’s about the same everywhere in the world: be polite, show interest and you will find the most welcoming and friendly people in the world in Morocco. 

If you should have more question further questions, just let me know. B’slama

I just got back from Morocco and every point you made is 100% accurate. Take cash, be aware, don't take pics, be careful in Medinas but also respect the country, people and their culture! Marrakech restaurant recs for Moroccan food, DAM (dejeuner at Marrakesh), La Pergola (for fusion Moroccan and great ambience! Fez - Cafe Clock in the Medina (historic building and very inexpensive Moroccan food), Palais Tijani in the medina - very authentic Moroccan food. In Dades Valley - highly recommend Riad Paradise for staying one night to break the journey to the Sahara. Yes get the luxury tents for extra $, so worth the serve and accommodations. In Fez visit the Tannery if you can stomach the smell of the carcass. Also don't miss Chefchaouen - a gem!
For anyone coming from Canada. Canadian ATM cards work in Al-Barid and BMCI banks. Go with Al-Barid, it has good conversion and no fee.

But nonetheless, you are still allowed to film, unlike many other nations! Just be respectful and don't film people without their permission.

Who exactly are “they”? This vague framing is misleading and unnecessary. Morocco does not need validation from unnamed critics, nor does it need to be defended against imaginary judgments. Using such a title is cheap clickbait and poor content practice. We all know that Morocco is a beautiful and safe country, based on reality and lived experience not on the opinions of an undefined “they.” Content should be honest, clear, and responsible. Be careful with misleading titles like this.

One thing you have to do as a tourist in Morocco is hamam, , ita a great and authentic experience, You won't regret it.
The Hammams aren't mixed-gender like Sona style in Europe, no!😁 . ; women and men are separated, women have their own hamam and all women they can see each other in public with bikini and men are not allowed to enter to women's hammam, because in Morocco it is shame to mix as Muslims gender diversity is forbidden in some places in Morocco like hammams and mosques but not restaurants or beachs 😊 or markets... so if you decide to test hammam adventure, you just need to bring your own shampoos, shower gel, and towel and A slightly rough fabric tool to scrape off your dead skin, because your body will release a lot of dead cells when you're wet and immersed in the steam and heat of the hammam for 30 minutes straight.. Don't forget to tell the cashier you want a masseur. It's really important; the masseur will stretch all your muscles. It's not a physiotherapist who will just massage your feet. Don't be afraid of him; he's not transgender, and he works in public, meaning you won't be alone. The hammam is a public place that's always busy, especially in the evenings on weekends, because it allows people to de-stress and recharge, like being reborn. Try it; you'll see. The price of a masseur doesn't exceed €10 for a 30-minute to 1-hour session, and the hammam entrance fee is no more than €5. You can stay in the hammam as long as you like, up to 5 hours at a time.

Two things. There is a difference between home cooked  standard size tagine and tagine served in a restaurant. Most restaurants precook and then finish it in a tagine for the last 10 minutes. That’s not a tagine but a stew. And quality meat is very expensive in the country. 100 mad is a good price for this size homemade. In some restaurants you will pay up to 250-300 mad if you want it with decent meat. Second, always haggle, you can even haggle when it comes to food (non official vendors). Normal price for a djellaba is 100-150 mad for decent quality. Don’t be ashamed, it’s normal to negotiate. Always check the stitching on traditional Moroccan clothing, they will try to sell something made in production as handmade. (My cousins advised me once, always but 2/5 of the price but I like to start with half the price 😂). Enjoy the trip.
It will take a few days to understand the vibe, but then....🎉 you will fall in love with it!  From the snowy mountains in Ifrane to the dunes of Merzouga!  ❤

Good to see you finding your feet quickly in Morocco. 
We were there in the same town a couple of years ago on the motorbike, though staying at a hotel for only one night, the following morning the typical town walkaround - it was very much quieter due to the Ramadan. 
Lovely people, courteous and very generous. 
Something one sometimes discovers about the Moroccans is how, while lacking in resources, they are incredibly resourceful - a mate of ours slid off on a narrow bend completely destroying the left hand pannier on his Honda NT, we collected the pieces and took them to a guy in a village where it was all stuck together again on top of a tin base, looked as good as new and twice as strong.
One thing to remember when you are offered merchandise and foodstuffs on the street is that you are expected to bargain with them; if they are asking 150 dirhams for a lovely blue turban they will be disappointed if you don't join the game saying you cannot possibly pay more than 30, you will end up getting it for 90 and everyone is happy.
Remember to watch the weather closely as soon as you reach mountainous areas, what I said about the snow still goes. 
Enjoy

I have seen many videos regarding Morocco. I have been there multiple times. I was not expecting will there be anything and enlightening. 

I have traveled to numerous countries and despite seeing things concerning, I will say many things happen in the US but are spun in the media differently. 

We are definitely blessed enough to have the greatest and best economy globally. But we have our own scammers outside and even inside government. Your house value may not go up but you will pay more in taxes because of appraisal. Gas prices fluctuate but don't fluctuate in Morocco. They may not like Christians in some countries but we can see the disparity with deportations. Nothing new under the Sun. They have gangs in malicious and we have corporation. Don't pay your criminals and goons will come looking for you. Don't pay your shakedown with corporations and government and someone will come looking for you. Our media just makes it look better. 

There are people that hit the lotto and make everyone think they can win also. There are people that get into bad situations overseas and people think it will happen to them. Most people who shut their mouth and lay low never see any problems. But if an American wolf with cameras anywhere they will see scammers. I have seen that enough times in every country. Even in New York City a tourist will get scammed if they walk around taking pictures.

As far as going on vacation, Morocco is a wonderful place. I have not seen the cobras and I did not go to the Atlas mountain but I will.

The best vacations are vacations that you will save money compared to staying in the United States.

Yes, Morocco can be very difficult to fly a drone in, but if you prepare, it's possible. You submit an official application and must specify exactly where you'll be filming. If you mention, for example, that you have a DJI and it automatically blocks you from flying in sensitive areas, it's also very important to share your YouTube channel. It's very unpredictable; sometimes you have everything in order and they still refuse, and sometimes you provide little information and they grant you permission. Another thing you can do if you're denied and still want some shots is ask a local drone operator to take a few shots for you. While you won't always have the drone with you and the freedom to take it everywhere, you can at least fill some spots for your YouTube video with content. Maybe next time you're in Morocco. Good luck and have fun!

This is the difference between an imported product and a locally produced one. Locally produced products are often priced closer to local purchasing power, especially in large producing countries. Local production is insignificant if it's limited and doesn't cover the domestic market. Imported products, on the other hand, are priced the same as in the international market, but the difference in local price is the government's ability to subsidize them to match local purchasing power. This applies to rich, middle-income, and poor countries. For example, the Gulf countries have vast oil wealth and small populations. Despite importing almost everything, prices are good considering their purchasing power and high wages. There's no secret to economics: rich countries subsidize imported products from their treasury. An exception is a country with a medium or small economy but a large and diverse domestic production. In this case, prices will be good even if the country isn't very wealthy. Local fruits and vegetables in Morocco are very reasonably priced compared to most countries because Morocco is a strong agricultural producer, exporting over $8 billion worth of agricultural products annually. Similarly, Morocco is a major producer of fish and grants fishing licenses to China, Russia, Japan, and the European Union. Generally, prices are reasonable in Morocco, and there is a great variety and quality. Of course, there are very expensive types of fish, but the small, surface-dwelling fish that are plentiful are reasonably priced.

زرت أغلب دول العالم على متن دراجتي "الهارلي ديڨدسن   غلايتر" منذ سنوات (على فكرة ثمنها يطعم قرية مصرية جائعة كاملة لمدة شهر...!) حتى في أدغال إفريقيا و الفلپين و أمريكا الاتينية، لم أصادف أي مشكل يذكر، إلى أن حللت بالإسكندرية، حيث تمت سرقة دراجتي بحي الورديان لما كنت في زيارة صديق مصري مريض لمساعدته...بعد شد و جذب و أخد و رد، تكلفت الشرطة المرتشية بالتوجه معي لمكان اخفائها بمرآب فندق شعبي  قرب محطة الأتوبيسات  بساحة 
النصر حيث حددت موقعها عن طريق جهاز  (دجي پي إيس) مخفي داخل مكبر الصوت لجهاز الستيريو لم ينتبه له اللصوص، حسنا... لكن لإستعادتها لم تستعمل الشرطة الإجرائات القانونية(لا حق لا قانون بمصر) بل طالبني ظابط الشرطة المكلف بالبحث برشوة كبيرة ليقوم بعمله طبقا "للقانون" زيادة على التمر و التهديد عن طريق بلطجية اقتحموا غرفة الفندق التي اكتريت ليلا، ليهددوني بساطور... احتلت عليهم كي يتركوني و شأني حتى الغد لأسحب النقود، رافقوني بعد ذلك إلى ساحة الرمل، ففررت منهم إلى داخل قنصلية دولة ديموقراطية (لم تكن آنذاك قنصلية مغربية بالإسكندرية)، اقتحمت باب القنصلية، اعتقلني الحراس، قابلت مسؤولا أمنيا بالقنصلية، حكيت له ما و قع، فبادر فورا بإبلاغ القنصل المحترم الذي ما إن عرف قصتي و  أنني مغربي، حتى بادر بالتدخل لصالحي... إلى أن استعدت دراجتي التي أوقعوا بها أعطاب كلفتني ثمنا باهضا بليماصول بقبرص، بعد أن رافقني مدير مكتب الهجرة بالقنصلية الشريفة، إلى أن استقلت باخرة مغادرة لميناء مدينة اللصوص المصرية نحو اليونان...!
الفساد و التعسف و الظلم ينخر العمود الفقري لمن يورثون المناصب و يستبيحون خزائن المال العام، و يولون الاراذل المسؤوليات...  .|

I'm enchanted by Morocco. I had a 13-day tour (flew into Casablanca, immediately off to Rabat, then Fez, Erfoud, Ouarzazate, Marrakesh, then back to Casablanca. It was all by bus, and though one of the segments was a near full day of travel, it was spectacular. I wish I could retire there, buy a house or own/operate a small riad in Marrakesh or Casablanca. The people are wonderful, very open, friendly, and kind. The weather is spectacular (and there's actually skiing available in the Atlas Mountains in winter), the food is wonderful, the culture is rich and fascinating (to me), there is so much to see, learn, and appreciate about Morocco.
